title: Substrate-driven topological engineering in plasmonic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chains
authors: Florian Herz, Alireza Naeimi, Svend-Age Biehs
abstract: We demonstrate the possibility of engineering the topological band structure of a plasmonic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain through the interaction with its electromagnetic environment. We find that the long-range interaction of the in-plane modes of the SSH chain with the surface plasmon polaritons of a planar substrate introduces a band hybridization connected to a change of the Zak phase. On the other hand, the short-range interaction with the substrate introduces a band touching, again with a change in the Zak phase. Surprisingly, this second mechanism enables the emergence of topologically protected edge modes for parameters which correspond to the topologically trivial phase for an isolated plasmonic SSH chain. We study these mechanisms by changing the chain-substrate distance and the dimerization parameter. Finally, we discuss the robustness against disorder and, as one example, the impact of the observed effects on the near-field radiative heat transfer along the chain. Our findings pave the way to the engineering of edge modes in plasmonic topological configurations via the coupling to a plasmonic environment.
summary: The paper demonstrates that the topological properties of a plasmonic SSH chain can be controlled by its interaction with a substrate. By tuning the distance to the substrate or the substrate's doping, one can induce topological phase transitions and create protected edge modes. These findings also show that such topological features can be used to modulate spectral radiative heat transfer.
main_problem: Investigating how the electromagnetic environment of a substrate can be used to engineer the topological band structure and induce topological phase transitions in a plasmonic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) chain.
main_result: The substrate interaction introduces two mechanisms—long-range band hybridization and short-range band touching—that can trigger topological phase transitions and enable the emergence of protected edge modes even in previously trivial regimes.
method: The study uses a Green's function approach within the dipole approximation and the quasistatic limit, applying Bloch's theorem and calculating the Zak phase and Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR).
model_or_system: A bipartite array of N identical InSb nanoparticles arranged in an SSH chain, placed at a distance z from a semi-infinite InSb planar substrate.
key_observables: Band structure, Zak phase, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), polarization (in-plane vs. out-of-plane), and spectral radiative heat flux.
important_parameters: Dimerization parameter (beta), chain-substrate distance (z), substrate carrier density (n_sub), and disorder strength (rho).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled using the dipole approximation in the quasistatic limit, and higher-order multipole moments are neglected.
figures_summary: Fig 1: System schematic; Fig 2: Eigenfrequencies and IPR; Fig 3: Infinite chain band structure and SPP interaction; Fig 4: Unit cell polarization; Fig 5: Nearest-neighbor approximation comparison; Fig 8: Eigenfrequencies/IPR vs distance; Fig 9: IP band structure; Fig 10-11: Band structure under disorder; Fig 12-14: Spectral radiative heat flux and robustness analysis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the plasmonic SSH system, details the mathematical framework for substrate-induced interactions, analyzes the two distinct topological engineering mechanisms (hybridization and band touching), evaluates the robustness of edge modes against structural disorder, and concludes with the implications for near-field radiative heat transfer.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how controlling the external electromagnetic environment (the substrate) can be used to manipulate topological edge states and near-field energy transport.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Sketch of the considered system. A bipartite SSH chain of nanoparticles of radius R with lattice constant d in each sublattice A and B and separation distance t within each unit cell is placed at a distance z in close vicinity to a semi- infinite planar substrate.

FIG. 2: Eigenfrequencies and IPR values for IP (a),(b) and OP modes (c),(d) for nsub = 1.34 × 1019 cm−3 (a),(c) and nsub = 1.36 × 1019 cm−3 (b),(d) at z = 500 for different β and a total of N = 80 particles.

FIG. 3: IP band structure for z = 500 nm and nsub = 1.36 × 1019 cm−3 for different β values. For each band, the Zak phases are given in the corresponding colors. The dashed lines correspond to the light lines in vacuum (black) and the surface polariton light line, i.e. the surface mode dispersion relation, of the InSb substrate (green) taken from Eq. (10).
FIG. 4: Polarizations of the unit cell |pA|2 + |pB|2 in x (solid) and z (dashed) direction for each band in Fig. 3 at β = 0.77.
FIG. 6: Polarizations of the unit cell |pA|2 + |pB|2 in x (solid) and z (dashed) direction for each band in Fig. 3 at β = 0.91.
